author: @mattmill30
text:
I was watching one of Sorin's videos on re-balancing and unlocking a Lenovo battery (ceOOJdX_Ag0) on the Electronic Repairs School channel, and I wondered whether it was possible to wirelessly charge batteries, without using an induction coil but rather, by placing the battery within an electrical field, such as beside a Tesla coil, which would avoid the need for invasive access which would void the warranty.
I don't know the relation between resonance frequencies and induction heating/charging, but could you enlighten on this train of thought?

author: @JohnWilkinsonTesla
text: What happens if you make a helical coil instead of a pancake coil? The Henries are the same so there would be an impedance match. It would look like a Tesla coil but the ends of the secondary would be connected to the LED. Would it raise the voltage at all?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Thank you - it's good to hear from someone who isn't just cynical lol

author: @mwm2929
text: This video reminded me of somthing in a Tesla documentary about the Wycliff Tower...  I think some high frequency lighting of florescent tubes using this coil would be in order for a future video.  You and I are cut from similar cloth when it comes to passion for science.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: you sir are too cynical - the world is full of magic and just down right strange and interesting things. It is the sheer wonder of the world that keeps me interested and experimenting. You are right it is induction but it is q coupled and at a distance - the efficiency is probably terrible but it took phone companies ages to come up with the idea of using this simple principle to charge mobiles - probably because they had no sense of the magic of the world

author: @otterpupp
text: it's called induction... why is this magical?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Good idea - upload if you will so i can see - I tried with magnets on the blades of a savonius with the coils static - some interesting results - i might resurrect the experiment and post it later.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Yeah I love it I have been working on this one for a bit now as a way of wirelessly sending energy
